claimed_contribution: The paper identifies when process versus outcome drives experience consumption evaluations by jointly considering consumer role (spectator vs participant) and self-construal (independent vs interdependent).
The paper provides process evidence that perspective taking explains why interdependents and independents weight process and outcome differently.
The paper extends the actor-observer framework by showing self-construal-contingent patterns: interdependents make more actor-like judgments as observers and more observer-like judgments as actors.
context: The paper examines leisure and entertainment experience consumption where process and outcome may conflict, such as sports games and a singing competition. Consumers are studied in either spectator roles or participant roles.
design_and_data: The authors report three between-subjects experiments. Study 1 (N = 437) used recalled sports experiences with manipulated experience type (positive outcome/negative process vs positive process/negative outcome), measured self-construal (Singelis scales), and role (spectator vs participant); analyses focused on respondents classified as predominantly independent (n = 85) or interdependent (n = 87), and included thought coding. Study 2 (N = 111) used an actual singing-competition task, manipulated self-construal via pronoun priming, manipulated role (perform vs watch), and manipulated type of experience by making positive outcome or positive process aspects salient. Study 3 (N = 230) added a perspective-taking suppression manipulation and manipulated actual process and outcome features of the singing experience, in a 2 x 2 x 2 x 2 design.
main_findings: Across studies, evaluations showed a consistent three-way interaction: as spectators, independents evaluated positive-outcome experiences more favorably while interdependents favored positive-process experiences, and this pattern reversed when they were participants.

In Study 1 thought protocols, independents focused more on outcome than process in spectator mode but more on process than outcome in participant mode, while interdependents showed the opposite pattern; interdependents also generated more perspective-taking thoughts overall.

In Study 3, suppressing perspective taking eliminated self-construal differences and produced a role x experience-type pattern: spectators preferred positive-outcome experiences, whereas participants preferred positive-process experiences.
one_line_summary: Across three experiments, the paper shows that whether process or outcome drives experience evaluations depends jointly on spectator versus participant role and independent versus interdependent self-construal, with perspective taking as the mechanism.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior work on outcome/process and actor-observer effects by showing that self-construal and consumer role jointly determine whether abstract outcome or concrete process information drives experience evaluations, and by demonstrating the role of perspective taking in this shift.
research_gap: Prior marketing research acknowledges the importance of outcomes in experiences, but gives less attention to process and does not clearly specify when consumers rely more on one versus the other, especially when process and outcome conflict. The paper addresses this contingency by proposing moderators of consumer role and self-construal.
research_question: When consumers evaluate experience consumption, how do process and outcome jointly influence evaluations when one is positive and the other is negative? The paper asks whether the relative weight of process versus outcome depends on consumer role (spectator vs participant) and self-construal (independent vs interdependent), and whether perspective taking explains these effects.
